samples.xml_description:
Implement a 4-bit unsigned number pipeline multiplier. Clock. Active-low reset. Multiplicand. Multiplier. Product. 4 The input signals (mul_a and mul_b) are extended by adding "size" number of zero bits at the most significant bit positions.
For example, if size = 4 and mul_a = 2, then mul_a_ext = 00000010.
This is done to prepare the input signals for the multiplication operation.
The multiplication operation is done using a generate block.
For each bit position i from 0 to size-1, the partial product is calculated as follows:
If the corresponding bit in the multiplier is 1, the multiplicand is left-shifted by i positions.
If the corresponding bit in the multiplier is 0, the partial product is set to 0 ('d0).
This is done using the following code:
for(i = 0; i < size; i = i + 1)
  if(mul_b[i] == 1'b1)
    partial_product[i] = mul_a_ext << i;
  else
    partial_product[i] = 'd0;
This can be interpreted as:
For each bit position i from 0 to size-1, the partial product is calculated as follows:
If the corresponding bit in the multiplier (mul_b) is 1, the multiplicand (mul_a_ext) is left-shifted by i positions.
For example, if mul_b[i] = 1 and mul_a_ext = 00000010, then partial_product[i] = 00001000 (left-shifted by 1 position).
If the corresponding bit in the multiplier is 0, the partial product is set to 0.
For example, if mul_b[i] = 0, then partial_product[i] = 'd0 = 0.
The add of partial products is done using registers.
For each bit position i from 0 to size-1, the sum of the partial products is stored in a register.
For example, if partial_product[0] = 00001000 and partial_product[1] = 00000100, then sum[1:0] = 00001100.
This is done using the following code:
for(i = 0; i < size; i = i + 1)
  if(rst_n == 1'b0)
    sum[i] <= 'd0;
  else
    sum[i] <= sum[i] + partial_product[i];
This is done using the following code:
always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n)
  if(rst_n == 1'b0)
    sum <= 'd0;
  else
    sum <= sum + partial_product;
This can be interpreted as:
On the positive edge of the clock signal (clk) or the falling edge of the reset signal (rst_n), the following is done:
If the reset signal (rst_n) is low, the registers are reset to 0.
If the reset signal (rst_n) is high, the registers are updated with the sum of the partial products.
For example, if sum[1] = 00001100 and partial_product[2] = 00010000, then sum[2] = 00011100.
The final product is calculated as the sum of the registers.
For example, if sum[3] = 00011100 and sum[4] = 00000100, then the final product = 00011100 + 00000100 = 00100000.
This is done using the following code:
always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n)
  if(rst_n == 1'b0)
    product <= 'd0;
  else
    product <= sum;
This can be interpreted as:
On the positive edge of the clock signal (clk) or the falling edge of the reset signal (rst_n), the following is done:
If the reset signal (rst_n) is low, the product output is reset to 0.
If the reset signal (rst_n) is high, the product output is updated with the sum of the registers.
